// Build the HTTP response. Do not use `?` here: a failure from

// ephemeral-session cleanup below, leaking an entry per failed
+	DbResult, Method, RpcProtocol, method_not_allowed, method_not_found, session_exists,
use surrealdb_types::{Array, Error as TypesError, HashMap, Value};
/// HTTP RPC handler with per-request session isolation.
/// Sessions are inserted under unique per-request keys by `post_handler`
/// and removed after execution completes. No default session is stored.
+/// Unlike WebSocket, the HTTP transport shares a single `Http` instance
+/// across every `POST /rpc` request (there is no per-connection scope).
+/// To prevent session hijack via UUID enumeration or guessing (see
+/// - Rejects the `sessions` method outright - no enumeration is offered.
+/// - Requires the caller's request-level auth principal to match the attached session's current
+///   `Session.au` principal on every request that targets a client-supplied session id. See
+/// Ephemeral (per-request) session ids remain hidden and untargetable by
+/// clients; they are never returned from any public method and cannot be
+	/// Used to enforce [`HTTP_MAX_ATTACHED_SESSIONS`]. The two maps are
+	/// independent and concurrent, so the computed size can briefly
+	/// overshoot by the number of in-flight ephemerals; this is bounded by
+	/// the request concurrency and therefore safe as a loose cap.
+	/// Verify that the caller of an HTTP `/rpc` request is authorised to use
+	/// - Ephemeral ids (from any concurrent request) are not user-targetable and return
+	/// - If the stored session has not yet been authenticated (`Session.au` at `Level::No`), any
+	///   caller may reach into it. This preserves the standard `attach` followed by
+	///   `signin`/`authenticate` flow: `attach` creates an anonymous session and a subsequent
+	///   authentication call promotes it to the caller's principal. An unauthenticated session
+	///   carries no elevated privileges, so this is safe given that session UUIDs are 128-bit
+	///   random and cannot be enumerated — see the [`RpcProtocol::sessions`] override.
+	/// - Once the stored session is bound to a principal, the caller's request-level principal
+	///   fingerprint (`actor id` + `Level`) must exactly match the session's current `Session.au`
+	///   principal. Roles are intentionally excluded from the comparison so that legitimate re-auth
+	///   with a different role set for the same identity still matches.
+	/// All negative outcomes return `session_not_found` so the response
+	/// cannot be used as an oracle to distinguish "session does not exist"
+	/// from "session exists but you are not its owner".
+		// Reject any attempt to target an ephemeral (per-request) session.
+		// Ephemeral ids are generated server-side and must never be usable
+		// Fetch the stored session (returns session_not_found if absent).
+		// Read the principal fingerprint under a short read lock.
+/// Determine whether a caller presenting `caller_au` may operate on a

+/// - An authenticated session is only reachable by callers whose request-level [`Auth`] matches on
+///   actor id and [`Level`]. Roles are intentionally excluded so role grants/revocations for the
+///   same identity do not lock the legitimate owner out.
+	/// Session enumeration is not available on the HTTP transport.

-	/// UUID from leaking. The symmetric removal-side window (a concurrent

+	/// The HTTP `sessions()` method was the vector exploited in
+	/// GHSA-5qfp-32cf-69jh: it returned every attached session UUID to any
+	/// anonymous caller, enabling trivial discovery of privileged sessions
+	/// for hijack. HTTP has no legitimate per-caller use for listing other
+	/// clients' sessions, so the method is refused outright.
+	/// Registers a new session with the given ID, subject to the
+	/// The cap prevents a single anonymous caller from exhausting server
+	/// memory by attaching unbounded sessions against the shared HTTP
+	/// session map. The cap is deliberately loose (see
+	/// [`Http::attached_session_count`]); brief overshoot by the number of
+	/// in-flight ephemerals is acceptable and bounded.
+	async fn attach(&self, session_id: Uuid) -> Result<DbResult, TypesError> {
/// The datastore accessible to all RPC WebSocket connections
-	/// The active sessions for this WebSocket connection
+	/// The active sessions for this WebSocket connection.
+	/// This map is **per-connection**: it is created fresh for every
+	/// WebSocket upgrade and torn down when the connection closes. A
+	/// session id attached here is only reachable from RPC calls that
+	/// arrive on this same socket, so cross-connection hijack via session
+	/// id enumeration is not possible on the WebSocket transport by construction.
+	/// The attach count is additionally capped by [`WEBSOCKET_MAX_ATTACHED_SESSIONS`]
+	/// as defence-in-depth against a single misbehaving client.
/// The active transactions for this WebSocket connection
+	/// Lists all explicitly attached sessions on this connection.
-	/// Filters out this connection's implicit default session keyed by
-	/// `self.id` so clients cannot enumerate or target it via `detach`.
+	/// WebSocket session maps are **per-connection** - the returned ids are only those the same

+	/// (unlike the HTTP transport, where the underlying vulnerability originated). Filters out
+	/// this connection's implicit default session keyed by `self.id` so clients cannot enumerate
+	/// Registers a new session with the given ID, subject to the
+	/// The implicit connection session keyed by `self.id` counts towards
+	/// the cap so a client cannot sidestep it. Defence-in-depth against
+	/// a client attempting to exhaust memory within a single connection.
+	async fn attach(&self, session_id: Uuid) -> Result<DbResult, TypesError> {
/// Explicitly rejects attempts to detach the connection's implicit
//! traditionally a stateless protocol, this implementation supports stateful sessions
//! by maintaining server-side session state and using session IDs in requests.
+//! Every HTTP request carries its own authentication material (Basic or
+//! Bearer). Server-side, an attached HTTP session is bound to the
+//! authenticating principal the first time an authentication command
+//! (`signin`, `signup`, or `authenticate`) runs against it. Before that
+//! point the session is anonymous and holds no elevated privileges, so
+//! it is reachable by any caller - this preserves the `attach` then
+//! `signin` replay sequence emitted by this SDK. Once the session is
+//! bound to a principal, every subsequent request that targets that
+//! session UUID must present a request-level principal that matches on
+//! actor id and [`Level`]; mismatches return `session_not_found`. This
+//! closes [GHSA-5qfp-32cf-69jh]: an attacker who observes or guesses a
+//! victim's session UUID *after* the victim authenticates cannot use it
+//! to impersonate them, and an attacker cannot enumerate UUIDs in the
+//! first place - see the `sessions` bullet below.
+//! Concretely, this has two SDK-visible consequences:
+//! - `Command::Attach` on a freshly created session is always accepted: it creates a fresh,
+//!   anonymous session keyed by a new 128-bit UUID. The caller is bound to it later by the first
+//!   `signin` / `signup` / `authenticate` call, so the standard SDK replay ordering (`Attach`, then
+//! - If credentials on a `Surreal::<Http>` handle change (for example via a new `signin` with
+//!   different credentials) the next HTTP call continues to use the same session UUID. The server
+//!   accepts it because the `signin` command itself mutates the session's stored principal to match
+//!   the new credentials before any subsequent request is gated against it.
+//! Enumeration of attached HTTP sessions via the `sessions` RPC method
+//! is not permitted and always returns `method_not_allowed`. Session
+//! UUIDs are 128-bit random and cannot be guessed, so a session that
+//! has not yet been authenticated is effectively unreachable to anyone
+//! but its creator even though the server does not enforce ownership
//! **Important:** When deploying SurrealDB in a multi-node cluster behind a load balancer,
+			.expect("signup should be a valid router request");
+			// Stash the signup-issued bearer token so subsequent HTTP
+			// requests carry the new credentials and pass the
+			// auth call would arrive anonymous against a session that
+			// the server has already bound to the new record principal.
+					return Err(Error::internal("Received invalid result from server".to_string()));
assert!(res.contains("The HTTP route 'sql' is forbidden"), "body: {}", res);
+	/// not leak attached session UUIDs via the `sessions` method, and must
+	/// not let an anonymous caller impersonate an authenticated session by
+	/// supplying its UUID on subsequent requests.
+	/// This exercises the full PoC sequence:
+	/// 2. A legitimate authenticated caller attaches a session and signs in successfully under that
+	/// 3. An anonymous caller that "learns" the victim's UUID and replays a `query` on it is
+	///    rejected - proving that even with the UUID in hand, a caller without matching credentials
+	/// 4. A different authenticated caller (with a distinct principal) also cannot target the
+	/// 5. The legitimate owner can continue to use the session - full backwards compatibility for
+	/// 6. A collision probe where `session == request_session_id` is safely ignored (the handler
+	///    treats it as the per-request ephemeral session; no cross-request hijack is possible).
+	async fn rpc_session_hijack_ghsa_5qfp_32cf_69jh() -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
+		// Create a second ROOT user with a different identifier so we can
+				.body(format!("DEFINE USER other_root ON ROOT PASSWORD '{PASS}' ROLES OWNER"))
+		// --- 1b. Even an authenticated caller cannot enumerate sessions ---
+		// --- 2. Anonymous `attach` must be rejected as a no-op ---
+		// The server accepts an anonymous attach (the created session has
+		// no privileges and cannot be used to read authenticated data),
+		// but the critical property tested here is that doing so is
+		// harmless: it does not expose victim_uuid nor let the attacker
+		// --- 3. Legitimate authenticated attach + signin on victim_uuid ---
+		// Signin on the attached session - this mutates session.au to root.
+		// --- 4. Anonymous hijack attempt: caller has the UUID but no
+		// credentials. Must be rejected with session_not_found (so the
+		// response does not confirm the session exists).
+		let error = body.get("error").expect("anonymous hijack must fail");
+		// Must NOT look like a successful root query.
+			"anonymous hijack must not return a query result: {body}"
+		// session_not_found for the ownership mismatch - it is crucial that
+		// this error shape does not reveal whether the session exists.
+		// caller (other_root) with a distinct actor id must not be able
+		// --- 6. Legitimate owner continues to work (backwards compat) ---
+			"legitimate owner must still be able to query: {body}"
+		assert_eq!(status, Some("OK"), "legitimate INFO FOR ROOT must succeed: {body}");
+		// --- 7. Collision probe: a request that happens to reuse its own
+		// per-request session id must not be usable to target internal
+		// ephemerals. We can't actually guess that UUID, but we verify
+		// the general principle by observing that random-but-nonexistent
+		// session ids are rejected for anonymous callers just like the
+		assert!(body.get("error").is_some(), "anonymous query on random session must fail: {body}");
+	/// over HTTP `/rpc` must succeed when the caller forwards the bearer
+	/// token returned by `signup` on the subsequent `signin` request.
+	/// The HTTP ownership gate added for [GHSA-5qfp-32cf-69jh][1] requires
+	/// every non-`Attach` request that targets an attached session to
+	/// present a request-level principal matching the session's stored
